name: Jerusalem
description: A hill city that became the political and religious center of ancient Israel under King David, and later the site of the Temple built by his son Solomon. It appears across nearly the entire biblical timeline, from its capture by David to its destruction by Rome in 70 AD.

name: Bethlehem
description: A small town a few miles south of Jerusalem, traditionally the birthplace of King David and, according to the Gospels, of Jesus. Its name means 'house of bread,' fitting for a town in fertile farmland.

name: Nazareth
description: A modest village in the hill country of Galilee, home to Jesus for most of his life before his public ministry began. In the first century it was small enough that it carried little prestige.

name: Sea of Galilee
description: A freshwater lake in northern Israel surrounded by fishing villages, where much of Jesus's early teaching and several of his disciples' careers took place. Its sudden storms are referenced in several Gospel accounts.

name: Jordan River
modern: Modern-day Jordan River, border of Israel/Jordan/West Bank
description: The river running the length of the region, crossed by the Israelites entering Canaan and later the site of Jesus's baptism by John. It functions repeatedly in scripture as a symbolic threshold between one stage of life and the next.

name: Mount Sinai
modern: Traditionally located in the southern Sinai Peninsula, Egypt
description: The mountain where, according to the book of Exodus, Moses received the Ten Commandments after leading the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt. Its exact modern location is debated among scholars.

name: Egypt (Goshen)
description: The fertile region where Jacob's family settled after Joseph rose to power there, and where their descendants were later enslaved before the Exodus. Egypt appears repeatedly in the Bible as both a place of refuge and of oppression.

name: Babylon
description: The capital of the empire that conquered Jerusalem in 586 BC and exiled much of its population. The decades-long Babylonian exile reshaped Jewish religious and communal life and forms the backdrop for several prophetic books.

name: Damascus
description: One of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, and the site where the apostle Paul (then known as Saul) is described as having a dramatic conversion experience while traveling to persecute early Christians there.

name: Ephesus
description: A major port city in Roman Asia Minor and home to one of the largest early Christian communities Paul helped establish, later the recipient of one of his letters preserved in the New Testament.

name: Rome
description: The capital of the empire that governed Judea during the New Testament period. Paul's letter to the Roman church and his eventual imprisonment there mark Rome as a recurring backdrop in the earliest spread of Christianity.

name: Mount Ararat
description: The mountain range where, according to Genesis, Noah's ark came to rest after the flood waters receded. It remains a significant symbolic landmark rather than a precisely pinpointed site.

name: Tarsus
description: A prosperous Roman provincial capital in Cilicia, in what is now south-central Turkey, and the birthplace of the apostle Paul according to the book of Acts. His upbringing in this significant trade and learning center is often cited to explain his fluency in both Jewish and Greco-Roman culture.

name: Antioch
description: A large, cosmopolitan city in Roman Syria where, according to Acts, the followers of Jesus were first called 'Christians.' It served as the base from which Paul and Barnabas were sent out on their missionary journeys.

name: Philippi
description: A Roman colony in the province of Macedonia, in present-day northern Greece, traditionally regarded as the site of the first Christian community established in Europe according to Acts 16. Paul's later letter to the Philippians, written from prison, reflects a warm relationship with this congregation.

name: Corinth
description: A major and wealthy port city in Roman Greece, known in antiquity for its commerce and its diverse, sometimes turbulent, cultural life. Paul spent an extended period establishing a church there and later wrote at least two surviving letters addressing disputes within the community.

name: Red Sea Crossing
modern: Traditionally located near the Gulf of Suez, Egypt
description: The traditional site where the book of Exodus describes the Israelites crossing a body of water on dry ground while fleeing Egypt's army. Its precise location is debated among scholars and archaeologists, some of whom argue the Hebrew phrase refers to a shallower 'Sea of Reeds' rather than the Red Sea proper.

name: Kadesh Barnea
modern: Modern-day northeastern Sinai Peninsula/Negev border area
description: An oasis in the wilderness south of Canaan where the book of Numbers describes the Israelites camping for much of their 40 years of wandering. It also served as the staging point from which spies were sent to scout Canaan before the Israelites' failed initial attempt to enter the land.

name: Patmos
description: A small, rocky Aegean island off the coast of Asia Minor where, according to the book of Revelation, its author John received the visions recorded there while in exile. Early Christian tradition identifies this John with the apostle, though scholars continue to debate the question of authorship.
